1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

1	\chapter{The Albanese universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174 / refined iter-175). This chapter is the A.4.d
6	% sub-row of Route~A (the positive-genus arm of
7	% \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the join of two preceding rows:
8	% A.3 (the identity component $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ of the Picard scheme together
9	% with its degree map; the relevant Picard-scheme representability content
10	% is recorded in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, A.2.c), and
11	% A.4.c (\cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}, Milne's
12	% Theorem~3.2 on rational maps from a nonsingular variety to an abelian
13	% variety). The output is Milne's Proposition~6.1 of Chapter~III~\S 6 of
14	% \emph{Abelian Varieties}: the Abel--Jacobi morphism
15	% $\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}$ at a marked $\bar k$-point
16	% $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$ is the \emph{Albanese morphism} of the pointed curve
17	% $(C, P_0)$. Any pointed morphism $\varphi \colon C \to A$ into an abelian
18	% variety with $\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A$ factors uniquely as
19	% $\varphi = \psi \circ \iota_{P_0}$ for a homomorphism of group schemes
20	% $\psi \colon \Pic^0_{C/k} \to A$.
21	%
22	% ROUTE DECISION (iter-174, refined iter-175). Two routes were on the table:
23	%   (i) MODULI / AUTODUALITY route. Pull the Poincar\'e bundle of $A$ back
24	%       along $\varphi \times \mathrm{id}_{A^\vee}$, classify the resulting
25	%       trivialised relative degree-zero line bundle by the Yoneda map
26	%       $A^\vee \to J$, dualise via the autoduality $J \cong J^\vee$
27	%       (Milne~III~6.6) and the principal polarisation. This route depends
28	%       on the autoduality of $J$, which gates on the \emph{theorem of the
29	%       cube} -- EXCISED from the project in iter-163.
30	%  (ii) SYMMETRIC-POWER route. Define $\widetilde\psi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g
31	%       C \to A$ from the symmetric assignment $(P_1,\ldots,P_g) \mapsto
32	%       \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)$, descend through the
33	%       birational morphism $\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J$, and apply
34	%       Milne's Theorem~3.2 (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) to
35	%       promote the rational map $J \dashrightarrow A$ to a regular
36	%       morphism. This is Milne's own proof of Proposition~6.1; it uses
37	%       \emph{only} proven \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} (Milne~I~1.1) and
38	%       \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne~I~3.2), and avoids the
39	%       cube entirely.

41	% draft (Poincar\'e-bundle pullback, moduli classifier of a trivialised line
42	% bundle) are retired; an ``Alternative route history'' \% NOTE block at the
43	% end of this chapter records the moduli wording for reference.
44	%
45	% This is the final wiring of the positive-genus Albanese-witness
46	% construction: the conclusion supplies, for each $\bar k$-rational point
47	% $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$, the universal-factorisation field
48	% $\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}\,P_0$ of an Albanese witness for $(C, P_0)$ in the
49	% sense of \cref{def:IsAlbanese} of \cref{chap:Jacobian}; together with the
50	% group-object / smooth / proper / geometrically-irreducible structure on
51	% $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ obtained at A.3 and the Abel--Jacobi morphism of
52	% \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} below, this yields a positive-genus
53	% \texttt{JacobianWitness} for $C/\bar k$ which then descends back to $k$ by
54	% Galois descent (\cref{chap:Jacobian}, sub-step C.2.f).
55	%
56	% The Lean target file is \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean}
57	% (not yet created); this chapter is the prover-ready specification.
58	
59	\section{Setup and statement}
60	\label{sec:albaneseup_setup}
61	
62	Fix an algebraically closed field $\bar k$ and a smooth proper geometrically
63	irreducible curve $C$ over $\bar k$ of genus $g = g(C) > 0$ (the positive-genus
64	hypothesis is essential: it is precisely the standing hypothesis of Milne's
65	\S III.6). Fix moreover a $\bar k$-rational point $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$ to serve
66	as the basepoint of the Albanese construction.
67	
68	By the A.3 row of Route~A (the identity-component construction applied to the
69	representable Picard scheme of
70	\cref{def:pic_scheme} in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) the
71	identity component $\Pic^0_{C/k} \subseteq \Pic_{C/k}$ is a smooth proper
72	geometrically irreducible $\bar k$-group scheme of dimension $g$; we write
73	$J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$ and let $\eta_J \colon \Spec \bar k \to J$ denote its
74	identity section, in the project's convention the chosen $\mathbf 1$-pointed
75	morphism that represents the unit element of the underlying group object on
76	$J$.
77	
78	The universal $T$-points of $J$ are degree-$0$ line bundles on $C \times_{\bar k}
79	T$, taken modulo line bundles pulled back from $T$ (\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback};
80	the identity component then refines this to the degree-zero subfunctor on the
81	\'etale sheafification). This moduli interpretation is what underlies the
82	Abel--Jacobi morphism $\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J$ of
83	\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The universal homomorphism $\psi \colon J
84	\to A$ corresponding to a pointed morphism $\varphi \colon C \to A$ is
85	constructed by Milne's symmetric-power route: the symmetric assignment
86	$(P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)$ defines a
87	regular morphism $\widetilde \psi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A$, the
88	birational morphism $\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J$ inverts to a
89	rational map $J \dashrightarrow A$, and the rational-map extension theorem
90	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes that rational map to the
91	desired regular morphism $\psi$.
92	
93	The goal of the chapter is to establish:
94	
95	\begin{theorem}
96	[Albanese universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ -- Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1]
97	  \label{thm:albanese_universal_property}
98	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese_universal_property}
99	  \uses{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism, def:symmetric_power_curve,
100	    lem:symmetric_product_av_map, lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian,
101	    lem:descent_through_birational_sigma,
102	    thm:rational_map_to_av_extends, def:pic_scheme}
103	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, doc p.~104
104	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
105	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
106	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let $P$ be a $k$-rational point on $C$.  The map
107	  %  $f^P\colon C\,\to\,J$ has the following universal property: for any
108	  %  map $\varphi\colon C\to A$ from $C$ into an abelian variety sending
109	  %  $P$ to $0$, there is a unique homomorphism $\psi\colon J\to A$ such
110	  %  that $\varphi=\psi\circ f^P$."
111	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, p.~104.}
112	
113	  Let $C$ be a complete nonsingular curve of genus $g > 0$ over an algebraically
114	  closed field $\bar k$, let $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$ be a $\bar k$-rational point,
115	  and write $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$, $\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J$ for the
116	  Abel--Jacobi morphism of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. Then for every
117	  abelian variety $A$ over $\bar k$ and every pointed morphism
118	  $\varphi \colon C \to A$ with $\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A$, there exists a unique
119	  homomorphism of group schemes $\psi \colon J \to A$ such that
120	  $\varphi = \psi \circ \iota_{P_0}$.
121	\end{theorem}
122	
123	The rest of this chapter develops the sub-lemmas
124	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}, \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
125	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}, \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
126	and \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}) that the proof rests on,
127	then gives the proof.
128	
129	\section{The Abel--Jacobi morphism at a marked point}
130	\label{sec:albaneseup_abel_jacobi}
131	
132	The first sub-lemma packages the construction and the elementary regularity of
133	the Abel--Jacobi morphism $\iota_{P_0}$ at the basepoint $P_0$. It is the
134	content gated on A.3 (existence of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ as a representable group
135	scheme).
136	
137	\begin{lemma}[Abel--Jacobi morphism at a basepoint]
138	  \label{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}
139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}
140	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, def:line_bundle_on_product}
141	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, the canonical map
142	  % $f^P\colon C\to J$ (introduction to Proposition~6.1), doc p.~104
143	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
145	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let $P$ be a $k$-rational point on $C$.  The map
146	  %  $f^P\colon C\to J$ has the following universal property: for any map
147	  %  $\varphi\colon C\to A$ from $C$ into an abelian variety sending $P$
148	  %  to $0$, there is a unique homomorphism $\psi\colon J\to A$ such that
149	  %  $\varphi=\psi\circ f^P$."
150	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, Summary 6.11, doc p.~107
151	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 113).
152	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
153	  % "SUMMARY 6.11.  Between $(C,P)$ and itself, there is a divisorial
154	  %  correspondence $\mathcal L^P = \mathcal L(\Delta-\{P\}\times C-C\times\{P\})$.
155	  %    Between $(C,P)$ and $J$ there is the divisorial correspondence
156	  %  $\mathcal M^P$; for any divisorial correspondence $\mathcal L$ between
157	  %  $(C,P)$ and a pointed $k$-scheme $(T,t)$, there is a unique morphism
158	  %  of pointed $k$-schemes $\varphi\colon T\to J$ such that
159	  %  $(1\times \varphi)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L$.  In particular,
160	  %  there is a unique map $f^P\colon C\to J$ such that
161	  %  $(1\times f^P)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L^P$ and $f(P)=0$."
162	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1 and
163	  Summary~6.11, \S III.6.}
164	
165	  Let $C$ be a complete nonsingular curve of genus $g > 0$ over $\bar k$, let
166	  $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$ be a basepoint, and write $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$. There is a
167	  unique regular morphism of $\bar k$-schemes
168	  \[
169	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J, \qquad
170	    Q \;\longmapsto\; \bigl[\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\bigr],
171	  \]
172	  sending the basepoint $P_0$ to the identity element $\eta_J \in J(\bar k)$
173	  and characterised on $T$-points by the formula
174	  $(1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}$, where
175	  $\mathcal L^{P_0} := \mathcal O_{C \times C}\bigl(\Delta - \{P_0\} \times C -
176	  C \times \{P_0\}\bigr)$ is the rigidified diagonal correspondence on
177	  $C \times C$ and $\mathcal M^{P_0}$ is the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence
178	  on $C \times J$.
179	\end{lemma}
180	
181	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (no separate verbatim source; the existence of
182	% $f^P$ is part of Milne's standing setup at the head of \S III.6 -- ``$J$
183	% will be its Jacobian variety'' -- and is the moduli-of-degree-0-line-bundles
184	% interpretation of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ packaged into Summary~6.11.)
185	\begin{proof}
186	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, def:line_bundle_on_product}
187	  By A.3, $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$ is the moduli scheme of degree-zero line bundles
188	  on $C$; concretely, for every $\bar k$-scheme $T$ its $T$-points are
189	  isomorphism classes of line bundles $\mathcal L$ on $C \times_{\bar k} T$
190	  whose fibre degree at every geometric point of $T$ is zero, taken modulo line
191	  bundles pulled back from $T$ (the universal property of the Picard scheme
192	  packaged in \cref{def:pic_scheme}; the identity component imposes the
193	  degree-zero condition).
194	
195	  Apply this to $T := C$ and the line bundle
196	  \[
197	    \mathcal L^{P_0} \;:=\; \mathcal O_{C \times_{\bar k} C}\bigl(\Delta -
198	      \{P_0\} \times C - C \times \{P_0\}\bigr),
199	  \]
200	  the rigidified diagonal correspondence on $C \times_{\bar k} C$. Its fibre
201	  over a $\bar k$-point $Q \in C(\bar k)$ along the second projection is the
202	  degree-zero line bundle $\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)$ on $C$, so $\mathcal L^{P_0}$
203	  is a relative degree-zero line bundle, hence a $C$-point of $J$. The
204	  representability of $J$ at $T = C$ produces the unique morphism
205	  \[
206	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J
207	  \]
208	  such that $(1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}$
209	  for the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence $\mathcal M^{P_0}$ on $C \times J$.
210	
211	  At the basepoint, restriction of $\mathcal L^{P_0}$ to $C \times \{P_0\}$
212	  gives $\mathcal O_C(P_0 - P_0) \cong \mathcal O_C$, the trivial line bundle,
213	  whose moduli class in $J(\bar k)$ is the identity element $\eta_J$. Hence
214	  $\iota_{P_0}(P_0) = \eta_J$. Uniqueness of $\iota_{P_0}$ is the moduli
215	  uniqueness clause of representability: any other morphism $\iota'_{P_0}$
216	  satisfying $(1 \times \iota'_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}$
217	  and the pointed condition agrees with $\iota_{P_0}$ on $T$-points for every
218	  $T$, hence equals $\iota_{P_0}$ by the Yoneda embedding.
219	\end{proof}
220	
221	\section{The $g$-th symmetric power $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$}
222	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g}
223	
224	The Albanese universal property is built from $C$ by taking the
225	$g$-th \emph{symmetric power} $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ -- the quotient of the
226	$g$-fold Cartesian power $C^g$ by the natural action of the symmetric group
227	$S_g$. We collect the definition here as the first sub-lemma block.
228	
229	\begin{definition}[Symmetric power of a curve]
230	  \label{def:symmetric_power_curve}
231	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.SymmetricPower}
232	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~3.1, \S III.3,
233	  % doc p.~94 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 100).
234	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
235	  % "For any variety $V$, the symmetric group $S_r$ on $r$ letters acts on
236	  %  the product of $r$ copies $V^r$ of $V$ by permuting the factors, and
237	  %  we want to define the $r$th symmetric power $V^{(r)}$ of $V$ to be the
238	  %  quotient $S_r \backslash V^r$.  The next proposition demonstrates the
239	  %  existence of $V^{(r)}$ and lists its main properties.
240	  %    A morphism $\varphi\colon V^r\to T$ is said to be {\bf symmetric} if
241	  %  $\varphi\sigma=\varphi$ for all $\sigma$ in $S_r$.
242	  %  PROPOSITION 3.1. Let $V$ be a variety over $k$.  Then there exists a
243	  %  variety $V^{(r)}$ and a symmetric morphism $\pi\colon V^r\to V^{(r)}$
244	  %  having the following properties:
245	  %    (a) as a topological space, $(V^{(r)},\pi)$ is the quotient of $V^r$
246	  %        by $S_r$;
247	  %    (b) for any open affine subset $U$ of $V$, $U^{(r)}$ is an open
248	  %        affine subset of $V^{(r)}$ and
249	  %             $\Gamma(U^{(r)},\mathcal O_{V^{(r)}}) = \Gamma(U^r,
250	  %             \mathcal O_{V^r})^{S_r}$
251	  %        (set of elements fixed by the action of $S_r$).
252	  %  The pair $(V^{(r)},\pi)$ has the following universal property: every
253	  %  symmetric $k$-morphism $\varphi\colon V^r\to T$ factors uniquely
254	  %  through $\pi$.
255	  %  The map $\pi$ is finite, surjective, and separable."
256	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~3.1, \S III.3, p.~94.}
257	
258	  Let $C$ be a complete nonsingular curve over an algebraically closed field
259	  $\bar k$ and let $g \ge 1$. The \emph{$g$-th symmetric power}
260	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ is a $\bar k$-scheme equipped with a finite surjective
261	  symmetric morphism $\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C$ characterised by
262	  the following universal property: for every $\bar k$-scheme $T$ and every
263	  symmetric $\bar k$-morphism $\varphi \colon C^g \to T$ (i.e.\ a morphism
264	  satisfying $\varphi \circ \sigma = \varphi$ for all $\sigma \in S_g$
265	  acting by permutation of the factors of $C^g$) there exists a unique
266	  morphism $\overline\varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to T$ with
267	  $\overline\varphi \circ \pi = \varphi$. As a topological space
268	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ is the quotient of $C^g$ by $S_g$, and for every open
269	  affine $U \subseteq C$ the open subset $U^{(g)} \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g
270	  C$ is affine with global sections the $S_g$-invariants
271	  $\Gamma(U^g, \mathcal O_{C^g})^{S_g}$.
272	\end{definition}
273	
274	\medskip
275	
276	\textbf{Mathlib status.} Mathlib has no formalised $g$-th symmetric power of
277	a scheme: only \texttt{Sym}/\texttt{SymmetricPower} for types and modules.
278	The corresponding scheme-theoretic construction must be supplied
279	project-side. The intended Lean construction follows Milne's recipe of
280	\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}: on affine open $U = \Spec A \subseteq C$
281	form $\Spec(A^{\otimes g})^{S_g}$, then glue via the standard affine
282	patching for an open cover $C = \bigcup U_i$. This sub-build is a
283	non-trivial dependency of A.4.d that the prover lane must absorb before the
284	main universal property closes; a \texttt{\textbackslash notready} marker is
285	appropriate on the corresponding Lean target until the construction lands.
286	For the proof of Proposition~3.1 itself, Milne refers to Mumford 1970,
287	\S II~\S 7, p.~66 and \S III \S 11, p.~112, for the patching details (the
288	quoted reference at the end of Milne's Proof on \S III.3, p.~94).
289	
290	\section{The morphism $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A$}
291	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_av_map}
292	
293	The second sub-lemma is the elementary fact that the symmetric assignment
294	$(P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)$ from
295	$C^g$ to $A$ descends through $\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C$. The
296	key point is that the addition morphism $A^g \to A$ is itself
297	$S_g$-symmetric because $A$ is commutative as a group scheme.
298	
299	\begin{lemma}[Symmetrisation of a curve-to-AV morphism]
300	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}
301	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerAVMap}
302	  \uses{def:symmetric_power_curve}
303	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
304	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
305	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
306	  % "PROOF.  Consider the map
307	  %     $(P_1,...,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.$
308	  %  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through $C^{(g)}$."
309	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
310	  \S III.6, p.~104.}
311	
312	  Let $\varphi \colon C \to A$ be a morphism into an abelian variety $A$
313	  over $\bar k$. The composition
314	  \[
315	    \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}
316	    \;\colon\; C^g \;\longrightarrow\; A^g \;\longrightarrow\; A,
317	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\; \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g),
318	  \]
319	  is $S_g$-equivariant (where $S_g$ acts on $C^g$ by permutation of the
320	  factors and on $A$ trivially). Consequently, by the universal property of
321	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ (\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}), there is a unique
322	  morphism of $\bar k$-schemes
323	  \[
324	    \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A
325	  \]
326	  such that
327	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ \pi = \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}$.
328	\end{lemma}
329	
330	\begin{proof}
331	  \uses{def:symmetric_power_curve}
332	  Since $A$ is a commutative group scheme, the $g$-fold addition morphism
333	  $\mathrm{add}_A \colon A^g \to A$ satisfies $\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau =
334	  \mathrm{add}_A$ for every $\tau \in S_g$ permuting the factors of $A^g$
335	  (this is the scheme-theoretic statement that summation in a commutative
336	  monoid is independent of the order of summands; on $T$-points it is the
337	  identity $a_1 + \cdots + a_g = a_{\tau(1)} + \cdots + a_{\tau(g)}$ in the
338	  abelian group $A(T)$). The diagonal action of $\tau$ on $\varphi^{\times
339	  g} \colon C^g \to A^g$ is also given by permutation of factors. Therefore
340	  \[
341	    (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}) \circ \tau
342	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\varphi^{\times g} \circ \tau)
343	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\tau \circ \varphi^{\times g})
344	    \;=\; (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau) \circ \varphi^{\times g}
345	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g},
346	  \]
347	  so the composition $\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}$ is symmetric
348	  in the sense of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}. The universal property
349	  of $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ produces the unique factorisation
350	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A$.
351	\end{proof}
352	
353	\section{The birational morphism $\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J$}
354	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_to_jacobian}
355	
356	The third sub-lemma is the birational identification of $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$
357	with $J$. After choosing the basepoint $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$, the cycle map
358	$(P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]$
359	defines a morphism $f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J$ which is
360	birational by Milne III~Theorem~5.1(a).
361	
362	\begin{lemma}[Birational morphism from $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ to $J$]
363	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}
364	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerToJacobian}
365	  \uses{def:symmetric_power_curve, lem:abel_jacobi_morphism, def:pic_scheme}
366	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~5.1(a), \S III.5,
367	  % doc p.~101 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 107).
368	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
369	  % "Let $f^r$ be the map $C^r\to J$ sending $(P_1,...,P_r)$ to
370	  %  $f(P_1)+\cdots+f(P_r)$.  On points, $f^r$ is the map
371	  %  $(P_1,...,P_r)\mapsto[P_1+\cdots+P_r-rP]$.  Clearly it is symmetric,
372	  %  and so induces a map $f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}\to J$.  We can regard
373	  %  $f^{(r)}$ as being the map sending an effective divisor $D$ of degree
374	  %  $r$ on $C$ to the linear equivalence class of $D-rP$.  The fibre of
375	  %  the map $f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}(k)\to J(k)$ containing $D$ can be
376	  %  identified with the space of effective divisors linearly equivalent
377	  %  to $D$, that is, with the linear system $|D|$.  The image of $C^{(r)}$
378	  %  in $J$ is a closed subvariety $W^r$ of $J$, which can also be written
379	  %  $W^r=f(C)+\cdots+f(C)$ ($r$ summands).
380	  %  THEOREM 5.1.  (a) For all $r\le g$, the morphism
381	  %  $f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}\to W^r$ is birational; in particular,
382	  %  $f^{(g)}$ is a birational map from $C^{(g)}$ onto $J$."
383	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~5.1(a) and the
384	  preceding paragraph, \S III.5, p.~101.}
385	
386	  Let $C$ be a complete nonsingular curve of genus $g > 0$ over $\bar k$,
387	  let $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$ be a basepoint, and write $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$,
388	  $\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J$ for the Abel--Jacobi morphism of
389	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The regular morphism
390	  $\iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J^g$ composed with the $g$-fold
391	  group-law addition $\mathrm{add}_J \colon J^g \to J$ descends through
392	  $\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C$ to a regular morphism
393	  \[
394	    f^{(g)} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; J,
395	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\;
396	      \bigl[\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)\bigr].
397	  \]
398	  The morphism $f^{(g)}$ is birational: it is dominant (its image is
399	  $J$, the $g$-fold sum $\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)$ exhausts
400	  $J$ since $\dim J = g$ and the iterated sum reaches full dimension), and
401	  its generic fibre is a single $\bar k$-point (a degree-$g$ effective
402	  divisor $D$ on $C$ with $h^0(D) = 1$ is the unique element of its linear
403	  system $|D|$; such divisors form a dense open subset of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
404	  C$ -- a consequence of $\dim |D| = 0$ generically on a curve of genus $g$
405	  by Riemann--Roch). Equivalently, $f^{(g)}$ is a birational morphism in
406	  the sense that there exist dense opens
407	  $U \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g C$, $V \subseteq J$ such that
408	  $f^{(g)}|_U \colon U \to V$ is an isomorphism.
409	\end{lemma}
410	
411	\begin{proof}
412	  \uses{def:symmetric_power_curve, lem:abel_jacobi_morphism, def:pic_scheme}
413	  Symmetry of $\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g}$ is the
414	  specialisation of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} to the abelian
415	  variety $A := J$ and the morphism $\varphi := \iota_{P_0}$; the universal
416	  property of $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ produces the regular morphism $f^{(g)}$.
417	  The closed-form description on $\bar k$-points,
418	  $f^{(g)}(P_1, \ldots, P_g) = [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]$,
419	  follows because $\iota_{P_0}(P_i) = [\mathcal O_C(P_i - P_0)]$ by
420	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} and the group law on $J$ is the
421	  tensor-product of degree-zero line bundles.
422	
423	  We verify birationality. The image of $f^{(g)}$ in $J$ is the iterated
424	  sum $\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)$ ($g$ summands), which is
425	  closed (it is the image of the proper morphism
426	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J$, $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ being proper because $C^g$
427	  is proper and $\pi$ is finite surjective). By the dimension count
428	  $\dim \mathrm{Sym}^g C = g = \dim J$ (Riemann--Roch and the standing
429	  hypothesis $g > 0$), the image $W^g \subseteq J$ has dimension at most
430	  $g$, and equality forces $W^g = J$ since $J$ is irreducible. Hence
431	  $f^{(g)}$ is surjective with $\dim$-equal source and target.
432	
433	  For the generic-fibre identification, Riemann--Roch
434	  $h^0(D) - h^1(D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ at $\deg D = g$ gives $h^0(D) - h^1(D)
435	  = 1$, so for a general $D \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)$ with $h^1(D) =
436	  0$ (the locus where $h^1$ jumps is closed of positive codimension on a
437	  smooth curve, by upper semicontinuity of cohomology), we have $h^0(D) =
438	  1$ and the linear system $|D|$ consists of $D$ alone. Therefore the
439	  fibre of $f^{(g)}$ over the generic point of $J$ contains a single point
440	  $D$, i.e.\ $f^{(g)}$ is birational onto $J$.
441	
442	  By \cref{def:pic_scheme} the underlying $\bar k$-scheme $J$ is the
443	  Picard scheme $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ obtained at A.3, so the moduli-theoretic
444	  reading of $f^{(g)}$ (effective divisor class $\mapsto$ degree-zero
445	  line bundle class via $D \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(D - g P_0)]$) is the
446	  same as the Yoneda image of the $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$-point
447	  $[\mathcal O_{C \times \mathrm{Sym}^g C}(\mathcal D_g - g\{P_0\} \times
448	  \mathrm{Sym}^g C)]$, where $\mathcal D_g \subseteq C \times \mathrm{Sym}^g
449	  C$ is the universal effective Cartier divisor of degree $g$ on $C$. We
450	  do not need this Yoneda repackaging for the proof of the Albanese UP --
451	  the symmetric-power route uses only the morphism $f^{(g)}$ and its
452	  birationality.
453	\end{proof}
454	
455	\section{Descent through the birational quotient $\sigma$}
456	\label{sec:albaneseup_descent}
457	
458	The final auxiliary lemma packages the two-step descent that promotes a
459	morphism on $C^g$ (after passing through $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ via
460	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} and inverting $f^{(g)}$ via
461	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}) to a regular morphism on
462	$J$. The output is a rational map $J \dashrightarrow A$, which is then
463	upgraded to a regular morphism via Milne's Theorem~3.2
464	(\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}). This is the unique step of the
465	proof that consumes A.4.c.
466	
467	\begin{lemma}[Descent of $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi$ to a regular morphism $J \to A$]
468	  \label{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}
469	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.descentThroughBirationalSigma}
470	  \uses{lem:symmetric_product_av_map, lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian,
471	    thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
472	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
473	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
474	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
475	  % "It therefore defines a rational map $\psi\colon J\to A$, which (I 3.2)
476	  %  shows to be a regular map."
477	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
478	  \S III.6, p.~104 (the sentence ``which (I~3.2) shows to be a regular
479	  map'').}
480	
481	  Let $\varphi \colon C \to A$ be a morphism into an abelian variety $A$
482	  over $\bar k$. Let $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A$
483	  be the symmetrised morphism of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} and
484	  $f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J$ the birational morphism of
485	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}. Then there is a unique
486	  regular morphism
487	  \[
488	    \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A
489	  \]
490	  such that $\psi \circ f^{(g)} = \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi$ on the dense open
491	  on which $f^{(g)}$ is an isomorphism (equivalently, $\psi$ is the unique
492	  regular morphism extending the rational map
493	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1} \colon J \dashrightarrow
494	  A$).
495	\end{lemma}
496	
497	\begin{proof}
498	  \uses{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian, thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
499	  By \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, there exist dense opens
500	  $U \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g C$ and $V \subseteq J$ with $f^{(g)}|_U \colon
501	  U \xrightarrow{\sim} V$ an isomorphism. Setting $\psi_0 \colon V \to A$
502	  to be the composition $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi|_U \circ (f^{(g)}|_U)^{-1}
503	  \colon V \to U \to A$ produces a rational map $J \dashrightarrow A$.
504	
505	  The target $A$ is an abelian variety and the source $J$ is smooth over
506	  $\bar k$ (it is itself an abelian variety, hence nonsingular). Milne's
507	  Theorem~3.2 (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) therefore promotes
508	  $\psi_0$ to a unique regular morphism $\psi \colon J \to A$ extending
509	  $\psi_0$ on $V$. Uniqueness of $\psi$ follows from the
510	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle: two regular morphisms
511	  $J \to A$ agreeing on a dense open are equal.
512	\end{proof}
513	
514	\section{Proof of the universal property}
515	\label{sec:albaneseup_proof}
516	
517	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
518	% "PROOF.  Consider the map
519	%    $$(P_1,\ldots,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.$$
520	%  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through $C^{(g)}$.  It
521	%  therefore defines a rational map $\psi\colon J\to A$, which (I 3.2)
522	%  shows to be a regular map.  It is clear from the construction that
523	%  $\psi\circ f^P=\varphi$ (note that $f^P$ is the composite of $Q\mapsto
524	%  Q+(g-1)P\colon C\to C^{(g)}$ with $f^{(g)}\colon C^{(g)}\to J$).  In
525	%  particular, $\psi$ maps 0 to 0, and (I 1.2) shows that it is therefore
526	%  a homomorphism.  If $\psi'$ is a second homomorphism such that
527	%  $\psi'\circ f^P=\varphi$, then $\psi$ and $\psi'$ agree on
528	%  $f^P(C)+\cdots+f^P(C)$ ($g$ copies), which is the whole of $J$."
529	\begin{proof}[Proof of \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}]
530	  \uses{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism, def:symmetric_power_curve,
531	    lem:symmetric_product_av_map, lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian,
532	    lem:descent_through_birational_sigma,
533	    thm:rational_map_to_av_extends, def:pic_scheme,
534	    thm:rigidity_lemma}
535	  The proof follows Milne's Proposition~6.1 verbatim, using the four
536	  sub-lemmas of this chapter:
537	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} (Abel--Jacobi morphism $\iota_{P_0}$),
538	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} (symmetrisation of $\varphi$ to
539	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A$),
540	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian} (birational morphism
541	  $f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J$), and
542	  \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} (descent of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
543	  \varphi$ to a regular morphism $\psi \colon J \to A$ via
544	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}).
545	  \begin{enumerate}
546	    \item \emph{Symmetrisation.} Consider the map $C^g \to A$ sending
547	      $(P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)$
548	      (Milne's typeset proof writes $\sum_i \psi(P_i)$ in this line, but
549	      the construction is the symmetrisation of the input $\varphi$; we
550	      use the unambiguous symbol $\varphi$ throughout). Since $A$ is
551	      commutative this map is $S_g$-symmetric. By
552	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} it factors through the
553	      symmetric power $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$, defining a regular morphism
554	      \[
555	        \widetilde \psi \;:=\; \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi
556	        \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A.
557	      \]
558	    \item \emph{Descent to a regular morphism on $J$.} By
559	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, the morphism
560	      $f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J$ is birational. The
561	      composite $\widetilde \psi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1}$ is, a priori,
562	      only a rational map $J \dashrightarrow A$. By
563	      \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} -- whose proof is an
564	      application of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne
565	      Theorem~I~3.2, rational map from a nonsingular variety to an
566	      abelian variety is regular) -- this rational map extends uniquely
567	      to a regular morphism
568	      \[
569	        \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A.
570	      \]
571	    \item \emph{Factorisation $\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi$.}
572	      Milne's identification: $\iota_{P_0}$ is the composite
573	      \[
574	        C \;\xrightarrow{\;Q \mapsto Q + (g-1) P_0\;}\;
575	        \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\xrightarrow{\;f^{(g)}\;}\; J,
576	      \]
577	      where the first arrow sends a point $Q \in C(\bar k)$ to the
578	      effective divisor $Q + (g-1) P_0 \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)$
579	      (its image under $f^{(g)}$ in $J$ is
580	      $[\mathcal O_C(Q + (g-1) P_0 - g P_0)] = [\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)]
581	      = \iota_{P_0}(Q)$, matching \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}). On
582	      $\bar k$-points,
583	      \[
584	        \psi(\iota_{P_0}(Q)) = \widetilde\psi(Q + (g-1) P_0)
585	          = \varphi(Q) + (g-1)\varphi(P_0) = \varphi(Q),
586	      \]
587	      using $\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A$ and that $\eta_A$ is the identity of
588	      $A$. Hence $\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi$ on $\bar k$-points,
589	      and therefore $\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi$ as morphisms of
590	      $\bar k$-schemes (both sides are regular morphisms $C \to A$ from
591	      a reduced separated scheme to a separated scheme, agreeing on
592	      dense $\bar k$-points, hence agreeing everywhere by the
593	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle).
594	    \item \emph{Homomorphism property.} The morphism $\psi$ sends
595	      $\eta_J$ to $\eta_A$: indeed,
596	      $\psi(\eta_J) = \psi(\iota_{P_0}(P_0)) = \varphi(P_0) = \eta_A$.
597	      A regular morphism of abelian varieties sending the identity to
598	      the identity is a homomorphism of group schemes, by Milne
599	      Corollary~I~1.2 (a consequence of the Rigidity Lemma
600	      \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, see
601	      \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, Corollary~1.2). Therefore
602	      $\psi$ is a homomorphism of group schemes $J \to A$.
603	    \item \emph{Uniqueness.} Suppose $\psi'$ is a second homomorphism
604	      with $\psi' \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi$. Then $\psi$ and
605	      $\psi'$ agree on the set-theoretic image $\iota_{P_0}(C) \subseteq
606	      J$. Both are homomorphisms, so they agree on the subgroup
607	      generated by $\iota_{P_0}(C)$ -- which contains the $g$-fold sum
608	      $\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)$ ($g$ copies). The
609	      $g$-fold sum is the image of
610	      $\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J$,
611	      which descends to $f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J$, which
612	      is surjective by \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}.
613	      Therefore $\psi$ and $\psi'$ agree on a dense open subset of $J$
614	      (in fact on all of $J$ as a set), hence everywhere by the
615	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle.
616	  \end{enumerate}
617	  This completes the proof.
618	\end{proof}
619	
620	\section{Wiring for the positive-genus Albanese witness}
621	\label{sec:albaneseup_witness_wiring}
622	
623	\cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} supplies the universal-factorisation
624	field $\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}$ of an Albanese witness for $(C, P_0)$ in the
625	sense of \cref{def:JacobianWitness} (\cref{chap:Jacobian}); the remaining
626	fields are supplied by A.3:
627	\begin{itemize}
628	  \item $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$ (the underlying scheme).
629	  \item $\texttt{grpObj}$: the group-object structure on $J$ obtained from the
630	    abelian-group structure on $\Pic^0_{C/k}(T)$ for varying $T$, refined to a
631	    group object in the category of $\bar k$-schemes (A.3, A.2.c group-scheme
632	    refinement).
633	  \item $\texttt{proper}$: properness of $\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec \bar k$ from
634	    the closed-immersion property of the identity component in the proper
635	    representing Picard scheme (A.3; cf.\
636	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_proper}'s discussion).
637	  \item $\texttt{smoothGenus}$: smoothness of relative dimension $g(C)$ from
638	    the deformation-theoretic tangent-space identification with $H^1(C,
639	    \mathcal O_C)$, which has dimension $g(C)$ by Riemann--Roch.
640	  \item $\texttt{geomIrred}$: geometric irreducibility of the identity
641	    component of a $\bar k$-group scheme locally of finite type (Kleiman
642	    Lem.~lem:agps and Prp.~prp:pic0; cf.\
643	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}'s discussion).
644	  \item $\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}$: the present
645	    \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}, applied at each
646	    $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$.
647	\end{itemize}
648	
649	The witness so produced is over $\bar k$. The Galois-descent step from $\bar
650	k$ back to the original base field $k$ -- packaged at
651	\cref{chap:Jacobian}, sub-step C.2.f -- closes the loop and supplies the
652	$k$-side Albanese witness consumed by \cref{def:Jacobian}.
653	
654	\section{Lean encoding}
655	\label{sec:albaneseup_lean_encoding}
656	
657	The Lean target file is the new
658	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean} (declared at the top of
659	this chapter). The five target declarations are:
660	\begin{itemize}
661	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}: the regular morphism
662	    $\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}$ of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}.
663	    The signature exposes the marked point $P_0$ as an explicit argument and
664	    the Picard-scheme construction $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ as an existing object.
665	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.SymmetricPower}: the scheme-theoretic
666	    $g$-th symmetric power $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
667	    packaged with the canonical projection
668	    $\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C$ and its universal property
669	    (factorisation of every $S_g$-symmetric morphism out of $C^g$). \emph{This
670	    declaration has no Mathlib analogue}: Mathlib's \texttt{Sym} /
671	    \texttt{SymmetricPower} cover types and modules only; the
672	    scheme-theoretic construction must be supplied project-side, following
673	    Milne's affine-and-glue recipe of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}.
674	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerAVMap}: the
675	    symmetrised morphism
676	    $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A$ produced by
677	    \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} from an input morphism
678	    $\varphi \colon C \to A$ into an abelian variety.
679	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerToJacobian}: the
680	    birational morphism $f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J$ of
681	    \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, together with the data of a
682	    dense open isomorphism $U \xrightarrow{\sim} V$ realising
683	    birationality. The signature exposes the basepoint $P_0$ and consumes
684	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}.
685	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.descentThroughBirationalSigma}: the
686	    descent lemma of \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma},
687	    consuming \texttt{symmetricPowerAVMap} and
688	    \texttt{symmetricPowerToJacobian} together with
689	    \texttt{Albanese.Thm32RationalMapExtension.extend\_to\_av}
690	    (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) to produce the regular
691	    morphism $\psi \colon J \to A$.
692	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese\_universal\_property}: the
693	    main theorem, the existence-and-uniqueness statement of
694	    \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}. The signature is
695	    \[
696	      \texttt{P\_0 : C(\bar k)} \;\to\;
697	      \texttt{A : AbelianVariety $\bar k$} \;\to\;
698	      (\varphi : C \to A) \;\to\;
699	      (h\varphi : \varphi \circ P_0 = \eta_A) \;\to\;
700	      \exists ! \, \psi \colon J \to A, \;\; \psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi
701	      \;\wedge\; \psi \text{ is a group homomorphism}.
702	    \]
703	\end{itemize}
704	
705	The proof body of \texttt{albanese\_universal\_property} consumes its four
706	sub-lemma exports plus \texttt{Albanese.Thm32RationalMapExtension.extend\_to\_av}
707	(\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) and Milne's Corollary~I~1.2
708	(\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} consequence, registered at
709	\cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}); the wiring follows the five-step proof
710	of \S\ref{sec:albaneseup_proof}.
711	
712	The Picard-scheme inputs (\cref{def:pic_scheme} of
713	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) are imported from
714	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean} (when that file
715	materialises along the A.2.c row) and the identity-component / degree-map
716	refinement is gated on the A.3 row (which does not yet have a dedicated
717	chapter; see ``Notes for Plan Agent'' below). At the time of writing, the
718	prover-side declaration body
719	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albane\-se\_universal\_property} should be
720	skeletoned as a \texttt{sorry} that consumes the A.3 instances as
721	hypotheses with named placeholders, and \texttt{Sym\-met\-ric\-Power} should
722	be skeletoned with a \texttt{notready} marker until the affine-and-glue
723	sub-build lands. The remaining sub-lemma bodies become routine once both
724	inputs are available.
725	
726	\section{Out of scope}
727	\label{sec:albaneseup_out_of_scope}
728	
729	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Albanese universal property
730	(\cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} = Milne Proposition~III~6.1) and the
731	four internal sub-lemmas of the symmetric-power proof
732	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism},
733	\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
734	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map},
735	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
736	\cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}), plus the wiring of these into
737	a positive-genus Albanese witness. The following adjacent content is
738	explicitly out of scope:
739	\begin{itemize}
740	  \item \textbf{Representability of $\Pic_{C/k}$ (A.2.c).} Proved in
741	    \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} as
742	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}; this chapter only consumes it.
743	  \item \textbf{The identity component $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ and the degree map
744	    (A.3).} The identity component refinement of the Picard scheme together
745	    with its degree map is the subject of a forthcoming chapter; this chapter
746	    consumes $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ as a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
747	    $\bar k$-group scheme of dimension $g$ but does not construct it.
748	  \item \textbf{Milne's Theorem~3.2 (A.4.c).} Proved in
749	    \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
750	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only consumes it
751	    inside \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} to promote the
752	    rational map $J \dashrightarrow A$ to a regular morphism.
753	  \item \textbf{The scheme-theoretic construction of $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$.}
754	    Mathlib has no $g$-th symmetric power of a scheme; the
755	    \texttt{SymmetricPower} target of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve} is a
756	    project-side sub-build (affine $S_g$-invariants
757	    $\Spec(A^{\otimes g})^{S_g}$ on each open affine $\Spec A \subseteq C$,
758	    glued by the standard open-cover patching of Milne~III.3
759	    Proposition~3.1 / Mumford~1970 II.7 \& III.11). The construction itself
760	    is a non-trivial dependency of A.4.d; this chapter \emph{specifies} the
761	    target but does not develop its construction.
762	  \item \textbf{The autoduality $J \cong J^\vee$ (Milne~III \S 6.6) and the
763	    moduli / Poincar\'e-bundle Albanese route.} The iter-174 draft of this
764	    chapter proposed a route via the Poincar\'e bundle of $A$ and the
765	    autoduality of $J$, which gates on the theorem of the cube. The cube
766	    was excised from the project in iter-163; the autoduality / moduli
767	    route is therefore demoted and replaced by the symmetric-power route
768	    of this chapter. A reference recording of the moduli wording is given
769	    in the ``Alternative route history'' \texttt{\%~NOTE} block at the end
770	    of this file.
771	  \item \textbf{Milne Proposition~III~6.4 / Remark~6.5 / Theorem~6.6} (the
772	    divisorial-correspondence universal property for $C \times C \to A$,
773	    the identification of $(J, F)$ with the Albanese variety in the sense
774	    of Lang, and the autoduality $J \cong J^\vee$). Not needed by the
775	    symmetric-power route.
776	  \item \textbf{Weil's reconstruction of $J$ from the rational law of
777	    composition on $C^{(g)}$ (Milne~III \S 7).} The project does not need
778	    this alternative construction.
779	  \item \textbf{The genus-$0$ case.} The standing hypothesis of this chapter
780	    is $g(C) > 0$ (Milne's standing hypothesis of \S III.6). The genus-$0$
781	    case of the Albanese witness is supplied by
782	    \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (\cref{chap:Jacobian}), routed through
783	    \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} and the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling
784	    shortcut; that construction is independent of and disjoint from the
785	    Pic$^0$-route of this chapter.
786	  \item \textbf{Generalisations to a non-algebraically-closed base $k$.}
787	    The chapter works over $\bar k$ throughout. The descent of the witness
788	    back to $k$ via Galois descent of morphism equality is the subject of
789	    \cref{chap:Jacobian}, sub-step C.2.f.
790	\end{itemize}

8	/-!
9	# Milne Theorem 3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian
10	variety extends (A.4.c)
11	
12	This file is the **A.4.c** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
13	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the single
14	theorem of Milne's *Abelian Varieties* §I.3, Theorem 3.2:
15	**a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends,
16	uniquely, to a regular morphism**. This is the input that the Albanese
17	universal-property build (A.4.d, `Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean`) consumes when
18	promoting the symmetric-product rational map
19	`C^{(g)} ⇢ A` to a regular morphism `J → A`.
20	
21	Milne's proof is two lines: combine Theorem 3.1 (the codim-$\geq 2$ extension
22	result for rational maps from a normal variety to a complete variety —
23	project-side `thm:codim_one_extension` of
24	`Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean`, A.4.a) with Lemma 3.3 (the
25	pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy structure for rational maps into a group variety
26	— project-side `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` of the same chapter). The
27	combination forces the indeterminacy locus of `f` to be simultaneously of
28	codimension `≥ 2` and pure codimension `1`, hence empty, so `f` is everywhere
29	defined.
30	
31	## Status (iter-175 Lane J file-skeleton)
32	
33	This file is the **iter-175 Lane J** file-skeleton: the single pinned
34	declaration carries the intended substantive type signature (matching the
35	blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in
36	`chapters/Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex` line 50) with a `sorry`
37	body. The body is iter-176+ work, gated on the sibling chapters
38	`Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean` (A.4.a) and
39	`Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean` (A.4.b) landing their substantive
40	statements.
41	
42	The 1 pinned declaration is:
43	
44	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
45	   — Milne Theorem 3.2: for `X` a smooth (= nonsingular) variety over an
46	   algebraically closed field `k̄`, `A` an abelian variety over `k̄`, and
47	   `f : X ⇢ A` a rational map, there exists a *unique* regular morphism
48	   `g : X ⟶ A` whose induced rational map equals `f`.
49	
50	## Note on type expressivity
51	
52	The signature is encoded against Mathlib's existing
53	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap` (a partial-map equivalence class on
54	dense opens) and `Scheme.Hom.toRationalMap` (the canonical "regular morphism
55	yields a rational map" coercion). The substantive claim is the existence
56	and uniqueness of a `g : X.left ⟶ A.left` with `g.toRationalMap = f`. The
57	type is non-tautological: the existence is the content of Milne 3.2;
58	uniqueness is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement principle
59	(`AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isDominant`).
60	
61	## Abelian-variety conventions
62	
63	Throughout the project (cf. `AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`, `Jacobian.lean`),
64	an **abelian variety over `k̄`** is encoded as an object
65	`A : Over (Spec (.of k̄))` carrying the four instances:
66	
67	- `[GrpObj A]` (group-object structure on the over-category),
68	- `[IsProper A.hom]` (complete),
69	- `[Smooth A.hom]` (nonsingular),
70	- `[GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]` (geometrically irreducible).
71	
72	A **nonsingular variety over `k̄`** (in the convention of
73	`blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex` §1) is an
74	object `X : Over (Spec (.of k̄))` carrying:
75	
76	- `[Smooth X.hom]`,
77	- `[GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]`,
78	- `[IsSeparated X.hom]`,
79	- `[LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]`,
80	- `[IsIntegral X.left]`,
81	- `[IsReduced X.left]`.
82	
83	These four-plus-two instances will normally be supplied by `inferInstance` at
84	the call site.
85

89	(250 LOC, 1 pin). Source: Milne, *Abelian Varieties*, §I.3, Theorem 3.2,
90	p. 17 (`references/abelian-varieties.pdf`, PDF page 23).

105	/-! ## Milne Theorem 3.2 — rational maps to an abelian variety extend
106	
107	Let `k̄` be an algebraically closed field. Let `X` be a nonsingular variety
108	over `k̄` (smooth, geometrically irreducible, separated, locally of finite
109	type, integral, reduced). Let `A` be an abelian variety over `k̄`
110	(a group object that is proper, smooth, and geometrically irreducible). For
111	every rational map `f : X ⇢ A` there is a *unique* regular morphism
112	`g : X ⟶ A` whose induced rational map equals `f`.
113	
114	Milne's argument (`Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex` §1.4, Theorem 3.2):
115	let `U ⊆ X` be a maximal open of definition of `f` and `Z := X ∖ U`.
116	By the codim-$\geq 2$ extension theorem (Theorem 3.1, project-side
117	`thm:codim_one_extension`) the closed subset `Z` has codimension `≥ 2`.
118	By the pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy lemma for maps into a group variety
119	(Lemma 3.3, project-side `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy`), `Z` is either
120	empty or of pure codimension `1`. Both conditions force `Z = ∅`, so `U = X`
121	and `f` admits a regular representative on all of `X`. Uniqueness is the
122	standard reduced-and-separated agreement principle (cf.
123	`AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isDominant`): two regular morphisms `X ⟶ A` that
124	agree on a dense open of `X` agree everywhere.
125	
126	Blueprint reference: `thm:rational_map_to_av_extends` (Milne, *Abelian
127	Varieties*, Theorem 3.2, §I.3, p. 17). The pin in the chapter is at
128	`Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex` line 50.
129	
130	iter-176+ proof skeleton:
131	1. Pick a `PartialMap` representative `φ : X.PartialMap A` of `f` (using
132	   `Scheme.RationalMap.toPartialMap`, which exists for `X` reduced and `A`
133	   separated; both hold from the abelian-variety + variety instances).
134	2. Show `φ.domain = ⊤` by combining `thm:codim_one_extension` (codim `≥ 2`
135	   complement) with `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` (complement empty or
136	   pure codim `1`).
137	3. Read off `g := φ.hom` (the morphism on the full domain) and verify
138	   `g.toRationalMap = f`.
139	4. Uniqueness: two `g₁, g₂ : X ⟶ A` with `g₁.toRationalMap = g₂.toRationalMap`
140	   agree on a dense open of `X`, so by
141	   `AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isDominant` and reducedness of `X` they agree
142	   everywhere. -/
143	
144	/-- **Milne Theorem 3.2 — a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an
145	abelian variety extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism.**
146	
147	For `X` a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, and
148	`A` an abelian variety over `k̄`, every rational map `f : X ⇢ A` has a
149	*unique* regular extension `g : X ⟶ A` (i.e. `g.toRationalMap = f`).
150	
151	This is the input the Albanese universal-property build
152	(`Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean`, A.4.d) consumes when promoting the
153	symmetric-product rational map `C^{(g)} ⇢ A` to a regular morphism
154	`J → A`.
155	
156	iter-176+: body combines `thm:codim_one_extension` (A.4.a; codim-$\geq 2$
157	extension to a normal variety target — here the abelian variety, which is
158	complete) with `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` (A.4.a; pure-codim-$1$
159	indeterminacy for maps into a group variety). The two combine to force the
160	indeterminacy locus empty; uniqueness is the standard agreement principle
161	for reduced `X` and separated `A`. -/
162	theorem extend_to_av
163	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
164	    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
165	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
166	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
167	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
168	    {A : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
169	    [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom]
170	    [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]
171	    (f : X.left.RationalMap A.left) :
172	    ∃! (g : X.left ⟶ A.left), g.toRationalMap = f := by
173	  sorry
174	
175	end RationalMap
176	
177	end Scheme
178	
179	end AlgebraicGeometry
