content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
133

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field $\bar k$ throughout. By a \emph{variety} over $\bar k$
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over $\bar k$
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over $\bar k$.
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism $\varphi_U \colon U \to Y$ defined on a dense open subset
54	$U \subseteq X$, where two pairs $(U, \varphi_U)$, $(U', \varphi_{U'})$ are equivalent
55	if $\varphi_U$ and $\varphi_{U'}$ agree on $U \cap U'$ (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map $f$
56	is \emph{defined at} a point $x \in X$ if there is some representative $(U, \varphi_U)$
57	with $x \in U$; the set of such $x$ is an open subset $\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X$,
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of $f$.
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of $X$?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on $X$ or $Y$ the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if $Y$ is complete (proper over $\bar k$), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-$1$ point of a normal source $X$ (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if $Y$ is a group variety, the difference map
75	    $\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}$ on $X \times X$ has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For $Y$ an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-$1$ extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's $\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}$ API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
94	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
95	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
96	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let $V$ and $W$
97	  % be varieties over $k$\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
98	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
99	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map $\varphi$ is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
100	  % $v$ of $V$ if $v \in U$ for some $(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi$. The set $U_1$ of
101	  % $v$ at which $\varphi$ is defined is open, and there is a regular map
102	  % $\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W$ such that $(U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi$ --- clearly,
103	  % $U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U$ and we can define $\varphi_1$ to be
104	  % the regular map such that $\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U$ for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
105	  % \varphi$."
106	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
107	  Let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ be a rational map between varieties over $\bar k$.
108	  The \emph{domain of definition} of $f$ is the open set
109	  \[
110	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
111	  \]
112	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of $f$. The
113	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of $f$ is the closed complement
114	  \[
115	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
116	  \]
117	  The map $f$ is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of $X$) if and only if $Z(f) = \emptyset$.
118	
119	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
120	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
121	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
122	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
123	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
124	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
125	  closedness of $Z(f)$ is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
126	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
127	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
128	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
129	\end{definition}
130	
131	\begin{definition}
132	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
133	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
134	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
135	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus}
136	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
137	  % "an open subset $U$ of $V$ whose complement $V \smallsetminus U$ has codimension $\geq 2$"
138	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
139	  % PDF page 22).
140	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map $\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W$ from a normal
141	  % variety $V$ to a complete variety $W$ is defined on an open subset $U$ of $V$ whose
142	  % complement $V \smallsetminus U$ has codimension $\geq 2$."
143	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
144	  conclusion).}
145	  Let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ be a rational map between varieties over $\bar k$.
146	  We say $f$ is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-$\geq 2$
147	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset $Z(f) \subseteq X$
148	  has codimension $\geq 2$ in $X$. Equivalently, no prime divisor of $X$ is contained in
149	  $Z(f)$; equivalently, every point $\eta \in X$ with $\codim_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1$
150	  lies in $\mathrm{Dom}(f)$.
151	
152	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
153	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
154	  conclusions about the codimension of $Z(f)$. Phrasing the codim-$\geq 2$ condition
155	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
156	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
157	  ($Z(f)$ has codim $\geq 2$ from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} $Z(f)$ is
158	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} $\Rightarrow$ $Z(f)$
159	  is empty) as a single line.
160	
161	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
162	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
163	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
164	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
165	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: $f$ is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
166	  $\eta \in X$ with $\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1$, $\eta \in
167	  \texttt{f.domain}$. The encoding matches the standing $\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
168	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of $X$ and the
169	  hypothesis ``no codim-$1$ indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
170	\end{definition}

172	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
173	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
174	
175	The codim-$1$ extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
176	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
177	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
178	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
179	
180	\begin{lemma}
181	  [Smooth $\Rightarrow$ DVR at every codim-$1$ point]
182	  \label{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
183	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing_dvr_of_codim_one}
184	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (the definition ``regular in codimension one'',
185	  % together with the immediately preceding remark that on a nonsingular variety every
186	  % local ring is regular) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
187	  % PDF page 147).
188	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textbf{Definition.} We say a scheme $X$ is \emph{regular in
189	  % codimension one} (or sometimes \emph{nonsingular in codimension one}) if every
190	  % local ring $\mathcal O_x$ of $X$ of dimension one is regular."
191	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The most important examples of such schemes are nonsingular
192	  % varieties over a field (I, \S5) and noetherian normal schemes. On a nonsingular
193	  % variety the local ring of every closed point is regular (I, 5.1), hence all the
194	  % local rings are regular, since they are localizations of the local rings of
195	  % closed points."
196	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $Y$ is a prime divisor on $X$, let $\eta \in Y$ be its generic
197	  % point. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{\eta, X}$ of $X$ at $\eta$ is a discrete
198	  % valuation ring with quotient field $K$, the function field of $X$."
199	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of regular in codimension one;
200	  the DVR consequence at a codim-1 point).}
201	  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over $\bar k$ and let $\eta \in X$ be a point with
202	  $\codim_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1$. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a
203	  discrete valuation ring with fraction field the function field $K(X)$ of $X$.
204	\end{lemma}
205	
206	\begin{proof}
207	  By smoothness, $\mathcal O_{X, x}$ is regular for every closed point $x \in X$
208	  (Hartshorne I.5.1). Localising a regular local ring at a prime ideal yields a regular
209	  local ring; in particular, for $\eta \in X$ with
210	  $\codim_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1$, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is regular
211	  of Krull dimension $1$. A regular local ring of Krull dimension $1$ is exactly a
212	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
213	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field $K(X)$ of $X$ by
214	  irreducibility of $X$ (the unique generic point of $X$ lies above $\eta$, and the
215	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).
216	
217	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
218	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing\_dvr\_of\_codim\_one} is the
219	  instance/lemma stating that $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a Mathlib
220	  \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing} under the typeclass set
221	  \texttt{[Smooth\,(\dots)\,X.hom]\ [IsIntegral X.left]} and the hypothesis
222	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ $\eta$ = 1}. The fraction-field identification with
223	  \texttt{X.functionField} is a downstream named instance
224	  \texttt{Scheme.fractionRing\_localRing\_eq\_functionField} (or whatever Mathlib name
225	  the prover chooses at commit \texttt{b80f227}). The regular-local-ring-of-dimension-1
226	  $\Leftrightarrow$ DVR equivalence is shipped in Mathlib as
227	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.isDiscreteValuationRing\_of\_dim\_one} (or analogous);
228	  the prover re-exports it rather than re-proving.
229	\end{proof}

231	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-$\geq 2$ extension}
232	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
233	
234	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
235	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-$1$
236	point.
237	
238	\begin{theorem}
239	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-$\geq 2$ extension to a complete target]
240	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
242	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, def:codim_one_indeterminacy, lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr,
243	        cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
244	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
245	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
246	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
247	  % \dashrightarrow W$ from a normal variety $V$ to a complete variety $W$ is defined
248	  % on an open subset $U$ of $V$ whose complement $V \smallsetminus U$ has codimension
249	  % $\geq 2$.}"
250	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
251	  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over $\bar k$, let $Y$ be a complete variety over
252	  $\bar k$, and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ be a rational map. Then the
253	  indeterminacy locus $Z(f)$ (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
254	  $\geq 2$ in $X$; equivalently, $f$ is codim-$1$-indeterminacy-free
255	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
256	
257	  Specialising further: if in addition $f$ \emph{is} codim-$1$-indeterminacy-free
258	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of $X$ is contained in $Z(f)$), then
259	  $\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X$ and $f$ extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
260	  \to Y$. The extension is unique because $X$ is reduced and $\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
261	  in $X$.
262	\end{theorem}
263	
264	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that $V$ is a curve. Thus we are
265	% given a nonsingular curve $V$ and a regular map $\varphi \colon U \to W$ from an
266	% open subset of $V$ which we want to extend to $V$. Consider the maps
267	% [diagram: $U \hookrightarrow V$, $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
268	% \subseteq V \times W$, $U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
269	% $V \times W$ to $V$ and to $W$]
270	% Let $U'$ be the image of $U$ in $V \times W$, and let $Z$ be its closure. The image
271	% of $Z$ in $V$ is closed (because $W$ is complete), and contains $U$ (the composite
272	% $U \to V$ is the given inclusion), and so $Z$ maps onto $V$. The maps $U \to U'
273	% \to U$ are isomorphisms. Therefore, $Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
274	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
275	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
276	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map $V \times W \to W$
277	% to $Z\ (\simeq V)$ is the extension of $\varphi$ to $V$ we are seeking.
278	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
279	% $U$ be the largest subset on which $\varphi$ is defined, and suppose that $V
280	% \smallsetminus U$ has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor $Z$ in $V
281	% \smallsetminus U$. Because $V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
282	% valuation ring $\mathcal O_Z$ with field of fractions $k(V)$. The map $\varphi$
283	% defines a morphism of schemes $\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W$, which the
284	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
285	% morphism $\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W$. This implies that $\varphi$
286	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets $Z$ in a nonempty set,
287	% which is a contradiction."
288	\begin{proof}
289	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, def:codim_one_indeterminacy, lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr,
290	        cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
291	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
292	
293	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-$1$ components.} Suppose for contradiction that $Z(f)$
294	  contains a prime divisor $W \subseteq X$ (i.e.\ a codim-$1$ irreducible component).
295	  Let $\eta \in W$ be its generic point, so $\codim_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1$. By
296	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a discrete
297	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field $K(X)$. Any representative
298	  $(U, \varphi_U) \in f$ has $U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset$ and so determines
299	  a $\bar k$-algebra map from the function field $K(Y)$ of $Y$ to $K(X)$: explicitly,
300	  $\varphi_U$ defines a morphism of schemes $\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y$ (since
301	  $\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))$ is the generic point of $X$). By the valuative criterion
302	  of properness applied to the complete variety $Y$
303	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
304	  extends to a morphism $\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y$. Standard
305	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood $V$ of $\eta$ in
306	  $X$ on which $f$ has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
307	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)$, contradicting $\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)$. Hence no codim-$1$
308	  component of $X$ lies in $Z(f)$, i.e.\ $Z(f)$ has codimension $\geq 2$.
309	
310	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-$\geq 2$ points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
311	  $\Rightarrow$ extension" half).} Suppose now that $Z(f)$ has codimension $\geq 2$
312	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce $Z(f) = \emptyset$ under the additional input
313	  that ``$f$ is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
314	  point $x \in Z(f)$ with $\codim_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2$. By
315	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
316	  $\mathcal O_{X, x}$, we have
317	  $\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2$. The sheaf
318	  $\mathcal O_X$ on the punctured neighbourhood $V \smallsetminus \{x\}$
319	  (any small affine neighbourhood $V \ni x$) has $H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0$
320	  and $H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0$ (the depth-$\geq 2$ characterisation of
321	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
322	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of $Y$ around the
323	  closure $\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}$ along $f$; the resulting sections of
324	  $\mathcal O_X$ on $V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)$ extend uniquely to sections on $V$,
325	  yielding a regular representative of $f$ on $V$. Hence $x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)$,
326	  contradicting $x \in Z(f)$. Therefore $Z(f) = \emptyset$ and $f$ extends to a
327	  regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X \to Y$.
328	
329	  Uniqueness of $\widetilde f$ is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
330	  principle: two morphisms $X \to Y$ that agree on the dense open $\mathrm{Dom}(f)$
331	  agree on all of $X$.
332	\end{proof}
333	
334	\begin{remark}
335	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
336	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
337	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
338	  Step~2 uses the depth-$\geq 2$ extension property at a codim-$\geq 2$ regular
339	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
340	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension $d$
341	  $\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay of depth $d$). For our application the source $X$ is a
342	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
343	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
344	
345	  Concretely, when $X$ is a smooth surface (e.g.\ $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ or
346	  $C \times C$ for a smooth projective curve $C$), the codim-$\geq 2$ closed points of
347	  $X$ are exactly the closed points of $X$ (because $\dim X = 2$), and at each such
348	  point $\mathcal O_{X, x}$ is regular of dimension $2$, hence Cohen--Macaulay of
349	  depth $2$. The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
350	  symmetric-product rational map $C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ along the Abel--Jacobi
351	  birational equivalence to $J = \Pic^0_{C/k}$.
352	\end{remark}
353	
354	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
355	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
356	
357	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target $Y$ is a group
358	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension $1$.
359	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension $\geq 2$'' conclusion,
360	this forces the locus to be empty.
361	
362	\begin{lemma}
363	  [Milne Lemma~3.3: pure-codim-$1$ structure of the indeterminacy locus into a group
364	  variety]
365	  \label{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
366	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme}
367	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, def:order_at_point, def:codim1_cycles}
368	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17
369	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
370	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Lemma 3.3.} \emph{Let $\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow G$
371	  % be a rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either
372	  % $\varphi$ is defined on all of $V$ or the points where it is not defined form a
373	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in $V$ (i.e., a finite union of prime
374	  % divisors).}"
375	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17.}
376	  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over $\bar k$, let $G$ be a group variety over
377	  $\bar k$ (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow
378	  G$ be a rational map. Then either $f$ is defined on all of $X$, or the indeterminacy
379	  locus $Z(f) \subseteq X$ is a closed subset of pure codimension $1$ (i.e.\ a finite
380	  union of prime divisors).
381	
382	  Equivalently: it is \emph{never} the case that $f$ has a non-empty indeterminacy
383	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension $\geq 2$.
384	\end{lemma}
385	
386	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Define a rational map
387	%   $\Phi \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow G$, $\;(x, y) \mapsto \varphi(x) \cdot
388	%   \varphi(y)^{-1}$.
389	% More precisely, if $(U, \varphi_U)$ represents $\varphi$, then $\Phi$ is the rational
390	% map represented by
391	%   $U \times U \xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U} G \times G
392	%   \xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}} G
393	%   \xrightarrow{m} G$.
394	% Clearly $\Phi$ is defined at a diagonal point $(x, x)$ if $\varphi$ is defined at
395	% $x$, and then $\Phi(x, x) = e$. Conversely, if $\Phi$ is defined at $(x, x)$, then
396	% it is defined on an open neighbourhood of $(x, x)$; in particular, there will be an
397	% open subset $U$ of $V$ such that $\Phi$ is defined on $\{x\} \times U$. After
398	% possible replacing $U$ by a smaller open subset (not necessarily containing $x$),
399	% $\varphi$ will be defined on $U$. For $u \in U$, the formula
400	%   $\varphi(x) = \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u)$
401	% defines $\varphi$ at $x$. Thus $\varphi$ is defined at $x$ if and only if $\Phi$ is
402	% defined at $(x, x)$. The rational map $\Phi$ defines a map
403	%   $\Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \to k(V \times V)$.
404	% Since $\Phi$ sends $(x, x)$ to $e$ if it is defined there, it follows that $\Phi$
405	% is defined at $(x, x)$ if and only if
406	%   $\operatorname{Im}(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subset \mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)}$.
407	% Now $V \times V$ is nonsingular, and so we have a good theory of divisors (AG,
408	% Chapter 12). For a nonzero rational function $f$ on $V \times V$, write
409	%   $\operatorname{div}(f) = \operatorname{div}(f)_0 - \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty$,
410	% with $\operatorname{div}(f)_0$ and $\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty$ effective divisors
411	% --- note that $\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty = \operatorname{div}(f^{-1})_0$. Then
412	%   $\mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)} = \{f \in k(V \times V) \mid
413	%   \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x)\} \cup \{0\}$.
414	% Suppose $\phi$ is not defined at $x$. Then for some $f \in \operatorname{Im}
415	% (\varphi^\ast)$, $(x, x) \in \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty$, and clearly $\Phi$ is
416	% not defined at the points $(y, y) \in \Delta \cap \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty$.
417	% This is a subset of pure codimension one in $\Delta$ (AG 9.2), and when we identify
418	% it with a subset of $V$, it is a subset of $V$ of codimension one passing through
419	% $x$ on which $\varphi$ is not defined."
420	\begin{proof}
421	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, def:order_at_point, def:codim1_cycles,
422	        lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
423	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
424	  fix a representative $(U, \varphi_U)$ of $f$.
425	
426	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
427	  \[
428	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
429	    \qquad
430	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
431	  \]
432	  via the composition
433	  \[
434	    U \times U
435	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
436	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
437	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
438	  \]
439	  where $\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G$ is the group-variety inverse and
440	  $m \colon G \times G \to G$ is the group law. The map $\Phi$ is rational by
441	  composition: its domain is at least $U \times U \subseteq X \times X$, which is dense
442	  because $U$ is dense in $X$.
443	
444	  \emph{Sub-step 2: $\Phi$-defined at $(x, x)$ $\Leftrightarrow$ $\varphi$-defined at
445	  $x$.} If $\varphi$ is defined at $x$, the explicit formula $\Phi(x, x) = e$ (the
446	  identity of $G$) shows $\Phi$ is defined at $(x, x)$. Conversely, if $\Phi$ is
447	  defined at $(x, x)$, openness of $\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$ in $X \times X$ produces an
448	  open set $W \ni (x, x)$ on which $\Phi$ is defined; by projection (taking the slice
449	  $\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W$ for some open $W' \subseteq X$, possibly shrinking
450	  $W'$ so $\varphi$ is defined on $W'$ as well --- $W'$ need not contain $x$), the
451	  identity
452	  \[
453	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
454	    \qquad u \in W',
455	  \]
456	  exhibits a regular representative of $\varphi$ at $x$. Hence $x \in
457	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)$ if and only if $(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
458	
459	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map $\Phi$
460	  pulls $\mathcal O_{G, e}$ back to a $\bar k$-subalgebra of the function field
461	  $K(X \times X)$:
462	  \[
463	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
464	  \]
465	  Because $\Phi$ sends $(x, x)$ to $e$ wherever it is defined, $\Phi$ is defined at
466	  $(x, x)$ if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
467	  \times X, (x, x)}$ (i.e.\ every local function on $G$ near $e$ has no pole at
468	  $(x, x)$ after pullback).
469	
470	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension $1$.} The variety $X \times X$ is
471	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over $\bar k$), so the Weil-divisor
472	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
473	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on $X \times X$. For any nonzero rational function
474	  $f \in K(X \times X)^\ast$ decompose its divisor as
475	  \[
476	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
477	  \]
478	  with $\mathrm{div}(f)_0$ and $\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty$ effective and
479	  $\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0$ (Milne's notation, matching the
480	  project's $\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y$ of
481	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: $\mathrm{div}(f)_0$ collects the prime divisors with
482	  $\ord_Y(f) > 0$ and $\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty$ those with $\ord_Y(f) < 0$). The local
483	  ring at $(x, x)$ can be characterised as
484	  \[
485	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
486	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
487	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
488	  \]
489	  Suppose $f \in K(X \times X)^\ast$ is not in $\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}$;
490	  equivalently, $(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)$. The pole divisor
491	  $\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty$ is a finite sum of prime divisors of $X \times X$ (i.e.\ a
492	  closed subset of pure codimension $1$ in $X \times X$). Its intersection with the
493	  diagonal $\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X$ is a closed subset of pure codimension $1$
494	  in $\Delta_X$ (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
495	  divisor of $X \times X$ either contains $\Delta_X$ or meets it in pure codimension
496	  $1$; on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
497	  applied to the local equation of $\Delta_X$ inside $X \times X$).
498	
499	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose $f \colon X \dashrightarrow G$ is not defined on all of
500	  $X$. Pick $x \in Z(f)$. By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
501	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})$ with $(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
502	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
503	  \Delta_X$ is pure codimension $1$ in $\Delta_X$, passing through $(x, x)$.
504	  Identifying $\Delta_X$ with $X$ via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
505	  closed subset of $X$ has pure codimension $1$ and passes through $x$. By Sub-step~2,
506	  every point of this codim-$1$ subset is a point where $\Phi$ is undefined on the
507	  diagonal, hence where $\varphi$ is undefined. Thus $Z(\varphi)$ contains a pure
508	  codim-$1$ closed subset passing through $x$; since $x \in Z(\varphi)$ was arbitrary,
509	  every irreducible component of $Z(\varphi)$ has codimension exactly $1$, i.e.\
510	  $Z(\varphi)$ is of pure codimension $1$.
511	\end{proof}
512	
513	\begin{remark}
514	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
515	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on $G$
516	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of $G$ as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
517	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety $X \times X$ (Hartshorne~II.6, or
518	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
519	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
520	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on $X \times X$. None of these has a characteristic
521	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
522	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
523	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
524	\end{remark}
525	
526	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
527	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
528	
529	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-$1$ ruling-out) goes
530	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
531	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
532	the extension fails at a prime divisor $W$ if and only if some affine coordinate of
533	$f$ around the image $\overline{f(\eta_W)}$ has a strictly negative order
534	$\ord_W(\cdot) < 0$. This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
535	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
536	
537	\begin{theorem}
538	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
539	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
540	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_iff_order_nonneg}
541	  \uses{def:order_at_point, def:codim1_cycles, def:indeterminacy_locus,
542	        lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
543	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, pp.~130--131 (the valuation $v_Y$ associated to a
544	  % prime divisor, and the characterisation of $\mathcal O_{X, \eta_Y}$ as the
545	  % subring of $K(X)$ on which $v_Y \geq 0$) (read from
546	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
547	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $Y$ is a prime divisor on $X$, let $\eta \in Y$ be its generic
548	  % point. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{\eta, X}$ of $X$ at $\eta$ is a discrete
549	  % valuation ring with quotient field $K$, the function field of $X$. We call the
550	  % corresponding discrete valuation $v_Y$ the \emph{valuation of $Y$}."
551	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let $f \in K^\ast$ be any nonzero rational function on $X$.
552	  % Then $v_Y(f)$ is an integer. If it is positive, we say $f$ has a \emph{zero} along
553	  % $Y$, of that order; if it is negative, we say $f$ has a \emph{pole} along $Y$, of
554	  % order $-v_Y(f)$."
555	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$ and the order map).}
556	  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over $\bar k$, let $Y$ be a variety over $\bar k$,
557	  let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ be a rational map, and let $W \subseteq X$ be a
558	  prime divisor with generic point $\eta_W \in W$ (so
559	  $\codim_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1$). Let $(U, \varphi_U)$ be any representative of
560	  $f$ with $U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset$. The following are equivalent:
561	  \begin{enumerate}
562	    \item $f$ is defined at $\eta_W$ (i.e.\ $\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)$, equivalently
563	      $W \not\subseteq Z(f)$).
564	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood $V \subseteq Y$ of
565	      the generic image of $f$ near $W$, every regular function $g \in
566	      \mathcal O_Y(V)$ pulls back to a rational function $\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
567	      satisfying
568	      \[
569	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
570	      \]
571	  \end{enumerate}
572	
573	  Consequently: $f$ is codim-$1$-indeterminacy-free
574	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
575	  $W \subseteq X$ and every affine open $V \subseteq Y$ as above, the pullback
576	  $\varphi_U^\ast(g)$ has $\ord_W \geq 0$ for all $g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)$.
577	\end{theorem}
578	
579	\begin{proof}
580	  \uses{def:order_at_point, def:codim1_cycles, lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr,
581	        thm:codim_one_extension}
582	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}$ is a
583	  DVR with fraction field $K(X)$ and uniformiser determined by $W$; by
584	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly $\ord_W$. The local ring at
585	  $\eta_W$ is therefore characterised inside $K(X)$ as the subring
586	  \[
587	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
588	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
589	  \]
590	  The rational map $f$ is defined at $\eta_W$ if and only if, for any affine open
591	  $V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y$ around the image $\overline{f(\eta_W)}$,
592	  the pullback $\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)$ lands in
593	  $\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}$: this is the standard local characterisation of where a
594	  rational map is regular at a codim-$1$ point of a smooth variety (it is the
595	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
596	  equivalent to $\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0$ for every $g \in A$, which is
597	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~$\Leftrightarrow$~(2) is therefore established.
598	
599	  For the consequence, $f$ is codim-$1$-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor $W$ is
600	  contained in $Z(f)$, which by the equivalence above is iff the $\ord_W \geq 0$
601	  condition holds for every prime divisor $W$.
602	\end{proof}
603	
604	\begin{remark}
605	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
606	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
607	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
608	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
609	  isolation, the abstract codim-$\geq 2$ statement
610	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} suffices and \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
611	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
612	  $\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}$ API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
613	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-$0$ classifying-map
614	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
615	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
616	\end{remark}
617

619	\label{sec:codim1_lean_encoding}
620	
621	The Lean target file is the new
622	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean}. The declarations to
623	introduce, in order:

650	    --- \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}. Signature (informal): given a smooth
651	    variety $X$, a group scheme $G$ over $\bar k$ that is a variety (smooth, finite
652	    type, separated), and $f \colon X \dashrightarrow G$ rational, conclude that
653	    $Z(f)$ is either empty or of pure codimension $1$ in $X$.
654	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_iff\_order\_nonneg}

659	
660	\paragraph{Mathlib readiness audit.} At commit \texttt{b80f227} the prover should
661	verify:
662	\begin{itemize}
663	  \item \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain} is shipped at
664	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; its complement
665	    \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} is a one-line definition.
666	  \item The valuative criterion of properness in Mathlib form is
667	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper.valuative\_criterion} (or analogous); the
668	    prover invokes this in Step~1 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s proof.
669	  \item Mathlib's smoothness predicate
670	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Smooth} bundles the regular-local-rings hypothesis; the
671	    DVR-at-codim-1 lemma reduces to ``regular local of dim 1 = DVR''
672	    (\texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.isDiscreteValuationRing\_of\_dim\_one} or similar).
673	  \item Mathlib's product-of-smooth-is-smooth instance,
674	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Smooth.comp\_pullback} / equivalent, is needed in
675	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}'s Sub-step~4 to apply the Weil-divisor
676	    machinery to $X \times X$.
677	  \item The local-cohomology vanishing in Step~2 of
678	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s proof
679	    ($H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0$ at a
680	    depth-$\geq 2$ point) is a substantial Mathlib gap as of \texttt{b80f227}; the
681	    prover's options are (i) prove the depth-$\geq 2$ extension property as a local
682	    lemma using the \texttt{Module.depth} / Auslander--Buchsbaum machinery of
683	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}, or (ii) reformulate
684	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} in the weaker ``surface'' form
685	    (where $X$ has $\dim 2$, so codim-$\geq 2$ means closed points only and the
686	    extension is the classical Hartshorne~III.7.1 ``smooth-surface point removal''
687	    argument). For the consumer \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} only
688	    the surface case is needed in practice (the symmetric product $C^{(g)}$ is
689	    smooth, and the Abel--Jacobi map's intermediate-step indeterminacy lives on a
690	    smooth surface inside $C^{(g)} \times C^{(g)}$).
691	\end{itemize}
692	
693	No new core axiom is required. The proof of \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is
694	purely Weil-divisor manipulation on a smooth variety and is the cleanest part of
695	the chapter; the substance of the Lean engineering is in
696	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s proof, both halves of which rest on Mathlib API
697	gaps the prover must either fill in or work around.

699	\section{Out of scope}
700	\label{sec:codim1_out_of_scope}
701	
702	This chapter packages \emph{only} the two extension inputs and the Weil-divisor
703	reformulation needed for A.4.a. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of
704	scope:
705	\begin{itemize}
706	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular $\Rightarrow$
707	    Cohen--Macaulay corollary.} Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
708	    as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this
709	    chapter only consumes them (in Step~2 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see
710	    \cref{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}).
711	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.2 itself.} The combination of
712	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} into the
713	    statement ``a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
714	    extends'' lives in \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
715	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only supplies the two inputs.
716	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
717	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the ``map between AVs is
718	    translated homomorphism'' family of results, and the
719	    $\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A$ / unirational$\dashrightarrow A$ constancy
720	    statements) is downstream of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not
721	    needed by A.4 in the project's current routing: the genus-$0$ base case is
722	    handled via the proven Rigidity Lemma + $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut
723	    (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}), and the positive-genus arm consumes only the bare
724	    Theorem~3.2 statement.
725	  \item \textbf{Weil-divisor degree on $X \times X$ for $X$ a curve.} The proof of
726	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} invokes the Weil-divisor apparatus on the
727	    smooth surface $X \times X$ but does \emph{not} need the degree map of
728	    \cref{def:divisor_degree} (which is curve-specific); only the codim-$1$
729	    decomposition $\div(f) = \div(f)_0 - \div(f)_\infty$ on a nonsingular surface is
730	    used. The bilinear degree formula on $X \times X$ for $X$ a curve (intersection
731	    numbers, Hartshorne~V.1) is out of scope.
732	  \item \textbf{Cartier divisors and the Cartier/Weil comparison.} On a smooth variety
733	    they coincide and the chapter uses Weil throughout; the Cartier formalism is not
734	    introduced.
735	  \item \textbf{Generalisation to non-algebraically-closed base fields.} The
736	    project's positive-genus arm runs over $\bar k$ throughout; descent of the
737	    extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would require
738	    Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
739	  \item \textbf{Resolution of indeterminacy by blow-ups} (Hironaka-style, for higher
740	    indeterminacy codimensions on a smooth variety with a non-group-variety target):
741	    out of scope. The combination of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and
742	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} for $G$ a group variety is sharper than any
743	    blow-up-based approach because it produces an extension on the original $X$
744	    rather than on a blow-up; no blow-up apparatus is needed.
745	\end{itemize}

8	/-!
9	# Milne Theorem 3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian
10	variety extends (A.4.c)
11	
12	This file is the **A.4.c** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
13	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the single
14	theorem of Milne's *Abelian Varieties* §I.3, Theorem 3.2:
15	**a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends,
16	uniquely, to a regular morphism**. This is the input that the Albanese
17	universal-property build (A.4.d, `Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean`) consumes when
18	promoting the symmetric-product rational map
19	`C^{(g)} ⇢ A` to a regular morphism `J → A`.
20	
21	Milne's proof is two lines: combine Theorem 3.1 (the codim-$\geq 2$ extension
22	result for rational maps from a normal variety to a complete variety —
23	project-side `thm:codim_one_extension` of
24	`Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean`, A.4.a) with Lemma 3.3 (the
25	pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy structure for rational maps into a group variety
26	— project-side `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` of the same chapter). The
27	combination forces the indeterminacy locus of `f` to be simultaneously of
28	codimension `≥ 2` and pure codimension `1`, hence empty, so `f` is everywhere
29	defined.
30	
31	## Status (iter-175 Lane J file-skeleton)
32	
33	This file is the **iter-175 Lane J** file-skeleton: the single pinned
34	declaration carries the intended substantive type signature (matching the
35	blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in
36	`chapters/Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex` line 50) with a `sorry`
37	body. The body is iter-176+ work, gated on the sibling chapters
38	`Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean` (A.4.a) and
39	`Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean` (A.4.b) landing their substantive
40	statements.
41	
42	The 1 pinned declaration is:
43	
44	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
45	   — Milne Theorem 3.2: for `X` a smooth (= nonsingular) variety over an
46	   algebraically closed field `k̄`, `A` an abelian variety over `k̄`, and
47	   `f : X ⇢ A` a rational map, there exists a *unique* regular morphism
48	   `g : X ⟶ A` whose induced rational map equals `f`.
49	
50	## Note on type expressivity
51	
52	The signature is encoded against Mathlib's existing
53	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap` (a partial-map equivalence class on
54	dense opens) and `Scheme.Hom.toRationalMap` (the canonical "regular morphism
55	yields a rational map" coercion). The substantive claim is the existence
56	and uniqueness of a `g : X.left ⟶ A.left` with `g.toRationalMap = f`. The
57	type is non-tautological: the existence is the content of Milne 3.2;
58	uniqueness is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement principle
59	(`AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isDominant`).
60	
61	## Abelian-variety conventions
62	
63	Throughout the project (cf. `AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`, `Jacobian.lean`),
64	an **abelian variety over `k̄`** is encoded as an object
65	`A : Over (Spec (.of k̄))` carrying the four instances:
66	
67	- `[GrpObj A]` (group-object structure on the over-category),
68	- `[IsProper A.hom]` (complete),
69	- `[Smooth A.hom]` (nonsingular),
70	- `[GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]` (geometrically irreducible).
71	
72	A **nonsingular variety over `k̄`** (in the convention of
73	`blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex` §1) is an
74	object `X : Over (Spec (.of k̄))` carrying:
75	
76	- `[Smooth X.hom]`,
77	- `[GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]`,
78	- `[IsSeparated X.hom]`,
79	- `[LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]`,
80	- `[IsIntegral X.left]`,
81	- `[IsReduced X.left]`.
82	
83	These four-plus-two instances will normally be supplied by `inferInstance` at
84	the call site.
85	
86	## References
87	
88	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex`
89	(250 LOC, 1 pin). Source: Milne, *Abelian Varieties*, §I.3, Theorem 3.2,
90	p. 17 (`references/abelian-varieties.pdf`, PDF page 23).

95	universe u

97	open CategoryTheory Limits

99	namespace AlgebraicGeometry

101	namespace Scheme
102	
103	namespace RationalMap

105	/-! ## Milne Theorem 3.2 — rational maps to an abelian variety extend
106	
107	Let `k̄` be an algebraically closed field. Let `X` be a nonsingular variety
108	over `k̄` (smooth, geometrically irreducible, separated, locally of finite
109	type, integral, reduced). Let `A` be an abelian variety over `k̄`
110	(a group object that is proper, smooth, and geometrically irreducible). For
111	every rational map `f : X ⇢ A` there is a *unique* regular morphism
112	`g : X ⟶ A` whose induced rational map equals `f`.
113	
114	Milne's argument (`Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex` §1.4, Theorem 3.2):
115	let `U ⊆ X` be a maximal open of definition of `f` and `Z := X ∖ U`.
116	By the codim-$\geq 2$ extension theorem (Theorem 3.1, project-side
117	`thm:codim_one_extension`) the closed subset `Z` has codimension `≥ 2`.
118	By the pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy lemma for maps into a group variety
119	(Lemma 3.3, project-side `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy`), `Z` is either
120	empty or of pure codimension `1`. Both conditions force `Z = ∅`, so `U = X`
121	and `f` admits a regular representative on all of `X`. Uniqueness is the
122	standard reduced-and-separated agreement principle (cf.
123	`AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isDominant`): two regular morphisms `X ⟶ A` that
124	agree on a dense open of `X` agree everywhere.
125	
126	Blueprint reference: `thm:rational_map_to_av_extends` (Milne, *Abelian
127	Varieties*, Theorem 3.2, §I.3, p. 17). The pin in the chapter is at
128	`Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex` line 50.
129	
130	iter-176+ proof skeleton:
131	1. Pick a `PartialMap` representative `φ : X.PartialMap A` of `f` (using
132	   `Scheme.RationalMap.toPartialMap`, which exists for `X` reduced and `A`
133	   separated; both hold from the abelian-variety + variety instances).
134	2. Show `φ.domain = ⊤` by combining `thm:codim_one_extension` (codim `≥ 2`
135	   complement) with `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` (complement empty or
136	   pure codim `1`).
137	3. Read off `g := φ.hom` (the morphism on the full domain) and verify
138	   `g.toRationalMap = f`.
139	4. Uniqueness: two `g₁, g₂ : X ⟶ A` with `g₁.toRationalMap = g₂.toRationalMap`
140	   agree on a dense open of `X`, so by
141	   `AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isDominant` and reducedness of `X` they agree
142	   everywhere. -/
143	
144	/-- **Milne Theorem 3.2 — a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an
145	abelian variety extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism.**
146	
147	For `X` a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, and
148	`A` an abelian variety over `k̄`, every rational map `f : X ⇢ A` has a
149	*unique* regular extension `g : X ⟶ A` (i.e. `g.toRationalMap = f`).
150	
151	This is the input the Albanese universal-property build
152	(`Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean`, A.4.d) consumes when promoting the
153	symmetric-product rational map `C^{(g)} ⇢ A` to a regular morphism
154	`J → A`.
155	
156	iter-176+: body combines `thm:codim_one_extension` (A.4.a; codim-$\geq 2$
157	extension to a normal variety target — here the abelian variety, which is
158	complete) with `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` (A.4.a; pure-codim-$1$
159	indeterminacy for maps into a group variety). The two combine to force the
160	indeterminacy locus empty; uniqueness is the standard agreement principle
161	for reduced `X` and separated `A`. -/
162	theorem extend_to_av
163	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
164	    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
165	    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
166	    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
167	    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
168	    {A : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
169	    [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom]
170	    [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]
171	    (f : X.left.RationalMap A.left) :
172	    ∃! (g : X.left ⟶ A.left), g.toRationalMap = f := by
173	  sorry
174	
175	end RationalMap
176	
177	end Scheme
178	
179	end AlgebraicGeometry

10	# Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)
11	
12	This file is the **RR.1** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
13	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geom-irred genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is

15

18	ground. This file is therefore **project-bespoke**, scaffolding the formal-sum data
19	type `Div(X) = ⨁_{Y ⊂ X codim 1} ℤ` on a Noetherian integral scheme `X` satisfying
20	Hartshorne's condition `(*)`, the principal-divisor homomorphism

22	proper curve over an algebraically closed field, the degree-zero of principal divisors
23	on a complete nonsingular curve (Hartshorne Cor. II.6.10), and the linear-equivalence
24	relation `D ~ D'`.
